verses.text: Job sighs for the months gone by, when God's lamp shone on him and God's friendship rested on his tent. He aches for lost nearness to God. The sweetest part of past blessing, to a godly heart, is the sense of God's presence.

Job sighs for the months gone by, when God's lamp shone on him and God's friendship rested on his tent. He aches for lost nearness to God. The sweetest part of past blessing, to a godly heart, is the sense of God's presence.

Then the Almighty was with him, his children around him, his way so rich it seemed to flow with cream and oil. Home and plenty crowned his days. Such gifts are precious, yet they were never the deepest of his joys.

Then the Almighty was with him, his children around him, his way so rich it seemed to flow with cream and oil. Home and plenty crowned his days. Such gifts are precious, yet they were never the deepest of his joys.

When Job took his seat at the city gate, the young withdrew, the aged rose, princes fell silent, and nobles held their tongue. He was honoured by all. God had given him a place of weighty respect among his people.

When Job took his seat at the city gate, the young withdrew, the aged rose, princes fell silent, and nobles held their tongue. He was honoured by all. God had given him a place of weighty respect among his people.

All who heard him blessed him, for he delivered the poor, the fatherless, and the dying, till 'the blessing of him that was ready to perish came upon me.' His honour rested on his mercy. True greatness is measured by kindness to the helpless.

All who heard him blessed him, for he delivered the poor, the fatherless, and the dying, till 'the blessing of him that was ready to perish came upon me.' His honour rested on his mercy. True greatness is measured by kindness to the helpless.

All who heard him blessed him, for he delivered the poor, the fatherless, and the dying, till 'the blessing of him that was ready to perish came upon me.' His honour rested on his mercy. True greatness is measured by kindness to the helpless.

He clothed himself in righteousness and justice, was 'eyes to the blind, and feet... to the lame,' a father to the needy, and broke the fangs of the oppressor. His goodness was active and brave. Real religion rescues the weak and confronts the cruel.

He had expected to die peacefully 'in my nest,' his days many as the sand, his strength ever fresh. He had felt secure in God's favour. None of us knows how soon settled comfort may be overturned.

Men waited for his counsel in silence, drinking in his words 'as for the latter rain.' His advice was prized like life-giving showers. God can make a faithful man a fountain of wisdom to many.

When he smiled on the doubtful they took heart; he sat as a king among his troops, yet 'as one that comforteth the mourners.' He led with both dignity and tenderness. The best leaders combine strength with compassion.
